story:
I watch a Popsicle melt into a rainbow puddle on the hot asphalt, its stick jutting out like a sad little flag. The heat pulls the colors into a slow swirl. I can’t look away. Maybe because Mama made me stay outside until the sun goes down. School’s out, and she says she can’t be fooling with me all day. My Skechers tap together as I lean against our faded white house. They’re holey, but Grandmama says if it still works, it ain’t trash.

The ice cream truck only comes once a week. If I had a few dollars, I’d buy the pink swirly cone with sprinkles and little chocolate crumbs. Pink is my favorite. I asked Mama once and she called me selfish, said things were tight. My mouth’s so dry it hurts. She won’t let me inside to use the bathroom or get a drink. I grip the overgrown grass to keep from crawling into the street and licking the sweet, sticky puddle like a dog. Shame makes my face burn. Hunger makes my stomach twist.

Across the road, Sally Mae and Rachel Lily play hopscotch, giggling, their cones already licked clean. I tuck my chin to my knees and pretend I don’t see them. I tried to play before. They didn’t like my smell or my clothes. Standing there, I feel like the Popsicle—melted and useless. A colorful mess no one wants.

When the sun finally drops, Mama lets me in. Papa comes home from the construction site, jaw tight, saying his boss might not pay him. Mama sighs and fusses. She says she needs to buy things. She says it’s a lot of work dealing with me. They’ve argued about money before. I don’t speak. Papa says kids should be seen, not heard. I yelp when he smacks my elbows off the table. “Sit right,” he growls. Mama looks at me like I ruined her day. “She won’t ever listen.”

The heavy feeling blooms in my chest, that Big Sad Sickness that fills every corner and won’t leave. I stare at mashed potatoes until my vision blurs. Mama is so pretty. I want to tell people how pretty she is. Maybe if I clean more, she’ll smile at me. I want to be as pretty as her someday. But I’ll buy my babies ice cream. Not too much, so their teeth won’t hurt, but enough so they know I love them.

At night I hold Mr. Fluffy, my old stuffed rabbit, and listen to the tree branch tick the window like a clock. I rub his ears until I fall asleep. The next day I stand where the Popsicle was. Only a splintered stick remains. I don’t know what I expected—of course it melted—but I thought the stain would be bigger.

“Freak!” Rachel Lily shouts. Sally Mae sneers. Their words land like stones. I want to play. I want to laugh. But I’m different. Weird. Gross. I turn away, wiping a tear, and lean against the house again.

I wish it hadn’t melted. It gave me something to wonder about. I don’t go farther than the yard. I got lost once after taking the wrong bus after school. Two older girls found me crying, and their mama called Papa from the number on my backpack. That was the only time Mama and Papa were nice to me. Mama cried. Papa hugged me tight. For a moment, we felt like a real family.

Now I wish I had a different family—one that doesn’t lock me outside, one that buys ice cream, one that gets me a pink swirly cone with extra sprinkles.
